high-rise retirement residence in Johnson City, 
Tennessee, also had a fire originating on the 
first level with all but two of the 16 fatalities 
occurring on the upper floors because of 
smoke movement via vertical shafts that 
included the elevator hoistway. Please note 
that in these two examples, the buildings were 
either protected only partially or not at all by an 
automatic sprinkler system. 
 
These provisions are intended to prevent the 
infiltration 
of 
smoke 
into 
the 
hoistway 
enclosure. As noted in Section 3006.1, there 
are other provisions in the code that have 
elevator lobby enclosure requirements or 
hoistway 
protection 
requirements. 
The 
provisions have several conditions where such 
protection is required.  
 
The primary condition, where “traditional 
hoistway opening protection” is required, is 
where an elevator shaft connects more than 
three stories. In addition, this will apply to any 
building that is not sprinklered throughout in 
accordance with NFPA 13 or NFPA 13R. Other 
vulnerable occupancies regardless of being 
protected by sprinklers must also provide 
hoistway opening protection. This includes 
Group I-1, I-2 and I-3 occupancies. Finally, 
because of the added concern of height as it 
affects stack effect, hoistways in high-rise 
buildings where the height of the hoistway 
exceeds 23 m must be protected in accordance 
with Section 3006.3. Note the importance of 
the measurement of the hoistway height. This 
provides some relief to elevators that only 
travel a couple of stories, where the concern for 
smoke spread is similar to that of a low- or mid-
rise 
building 
protected 
throughout 
with 
sprinklers.  
 
There are several exceptions. The first does 
not require hoistway opening protection where 
the elevator is located in an open parking 
garage. This is consistent with Section 
712.1.10.2 . The concept is that an open 
parking garage has plenty of ventilation and 
various components therein, such as ramps 
and duct systems, do not need shaft protection.  
 
Exception 2 does not require hoistway opening 
protection at the level of exit discharge. This is 
only permitted where the level of exit discharge 
is protected with sprinklers. It should be noted 
that there can be multiple levels of exit 
discharge. As currently written, this can be 
applicable for any of those levels. The 
exception is notable in that it is conditioned on 
only the level of exit discharge, not the entire 
building being sprinklered. Fully sprinklered 
buildings would be relieved of the elevator 
lobby requirements on the floor of exit 
discharge.  
 
Exception 3 allows hoistway openings that 
open to the exterior. Such locations will not 
allow smoke to build up, thus reducing the 
chances of smoke entering the hoistway. A 
large stadium may have conditions where the 
elevator opens to the exterior.  
 
Although a specific exception for atriums is not 
provided, Section 712.1.7 is often considered 
to allow elevator hoistways located entirely 
within the atrium to be unenclosed. If an 
elevator is not required to have a fire-
resistance-rated enclosure, it serves no 
purpose to require a fire-resistance-rated 
elevator 
lobby 
enclosure 
or 
hoistway 
protection.  
3006.2.1  
Rated corridors.  Where corridors 
are required to be fire-resistance rated in 
accordance with Section 1020.2 , elevator hoistway 
openings shall be protected in accordance with 
Section 3006.3 .
pages.commentary:
This section requires protection of elevator 
hoistway openings based on the requirement 
for rated corridor construction. To further clarify 
this intent, Section 
1020.2.1 has been 
referenced as a pointer to these specific lobby 
requirements. It should be noted that this 
requirement has limited application. The 
following is a list of the buildings not already 
addressed by Section 3006.2 that are required 
to have rated corridors:

❖ Unsprinklered buildings: 
• 
Group A, B, E, F, M, S and U occupancies 
with an occupant load served by a corridor 
greater than 30. 
• 
Hoistways connecting only 3 stories.

❖ Sprinklered buildings: 
• 
Group R occupancies greater than 10 
occupants served by a corridor (1/2 hr) 
• 
Group H1, H-2, H-3 occupancies (1 hr) 
• 
Group H4 and H5 occupancies greater than 
30 served by a corridor (1 hr) 
• 
Non-high-rise buildings 
3006.3  Hoistway opening protection. Where 
Section 3006.2 requires protection of the elevator 
hoistway door opening, the protection shall be 
provided by one of the following: 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1956 
 
1 
An enclosed elevator lobby shall be provided 
at each floor to separate the elevator hoistway 
shaft enclosure doors from each floor by fire 
partitions in accordance with Section 708. In 
addition, doors protecting openings in the 
elevator lobby enclosure walls shall comply 
with Section 716.2.2.1 as required for 
corridor walls. Penetrations of the enclosed 
elevator lobby by ducts and air transfer 
openings shall be protected as required for 
corridors in accordance with 
Section 
717.5.4.1 . 
2 
An enclosed elevator lobby shall be provided 
at each floor to separate the elevator hoistway 
shaft enclosure doors from each floor by 
smoke partitions in accordance with Section 
710 where the building is equipped 
throughout with an automatic sprinkler 
system installed in accordance with Section 
903.3.1.1 or 903.3.1.2 . In addition, doors 
protecting openings in the smoke partitions 
shall comply with Sections 710.5.2.2 , 
710.5.2.3  and 716.2.6.1 . Penetrations of the 
enclosed elevator lobby by ducts and air 
transfer openings shall be protected as 
required for corridors in accordance with 
Section 717.5.4.1 .  
3 
Additional doors shall be provided at each 
elevator 
hoistway 
door 
opening 
in 
accordance with Section 3002.6 . Such door 
shall comply with the smoke and draft 
control door assembly requirements in 
Section 
716.2.2.1.1 
when 
tested 
in 
accordance with UL 1784 without an 
artificial bottom seal.  
4 
The elevator hoistway shall be pressurized in 
accordance with Section 909.21 .  
❖ 
Section 3006.2 sets out where hoistway 
opening protection is required. From there, 
Section 3006.3 provides the various ways this 
can be accomplished.  
 
The first option is to provide a lobby enclosure 
that separates the hoistway opening from the 
rest of the building by 1-hour fire-resistance-
rated partitions constructed as fire partitions. 
As a point of difference, the chute access 
rooms 
require 
fire 
barrier 
construction, 
whereas the elevator lobby requirements are 
for fire partition construction. Unlike a chute 
access room, an elevator lobby enclosure does 
not require a horizontal assembly for the 
floor/ceiling assembly. This section references 
Sections 716.2.2.1 and 717.5.4.1 for door 
openings and duct openings. Therefore, the 
elevator lobby separation requires the 20-
minute doors to be smoke- and draft-control 
doors (Section 716.2.2.1 ) and also requires 
duct penetrations to have smoke and fire 
dampers (Section 717.5.4.1 ) unless exempted 
by those sections. The purpose of the special 
opening protectives in the elevator lobby 
enclosures is to delay or prevent the vertical 
spread of smoke to other floors through the 
hoistway doors and the shaft.  
 
The second option is also an enclosed elevator 
lobby. However, unlike Option 1, it allows non 
fire resistance-rated smoke partitions in 
accordance with Section 710 to enclose the 
elevator lobby. This option requires either an 
NFPA 13 or 13R sprinkler system. The door 
openings in the lobby enclosure must also 
meet the air leakage rate in Section 716.2.2.1.1 
and must be self- or automatic closing (Section 
716.2.6.1 ). Any duct penetrations through the 
smoke partitions must also be provided with 
smoke dampers in accordance with Section 
717.5.4.1 .  
 
Option 3 eliminates the need for an enclosed 
lobby, but still requires the additional layer of 
fire resistance and smoke control. Doors in 
addition to the normal hoistway door are 
provided in front of the hoistway opening. 
These doors must meet two requirements. 
First, 
they must 
be 
easily 
opened 
in 
accordance with Section 3002.6 . This ensures 
that if the doors do close, they can be opened 
by someone who arrives at that level on the 
elevator, such as fire department personnel. 
This is very important if the doors are horizontal 
power-operated doors, such as those in 
Section 1010.3.3 of the code. Section 3002.6 
states that the doors shall be readily openable 
from the car side without a key, tool, special 
knowledge or effort. The second requirement is 
the testing of the additional door or doors. They 
must be tested in accordance with UL 1784. 
This test is titled “Air Leakage Tests of Door 
Assemblies.” This test does not provide a fire-
resistance rating for the door. It also has no 
failure criteria as it only measures the leakage 
rate. The leakage rate for smoke and draft 
control doors in Section 716.2.2.1.1 is a 
maximum of 15.24 liters per second per square 
meter of door opening at 25 pascals   pressure. 
It is assumed that they must meet the leakage 
rate requirements in Section 716.2.2.1.1 and 
they must be identified as smoke and draft 
control doors in accordance with Section 
716.2.9.3 .  
 
Option 
4 
allows 
elevator 
hoistway 
pressurization to be used in lieu of enclosed 
elevator lobbies in all buildings, regardless of 
height. This is similar to pressurization of 
-

710 where the building is equipped

903.3.1.1 or 903.3.1.2 . In addition, doors 
protecting openings in the smoke partitions 
shall comply with Sections 710.5.2.2 , 
710.5.2.3  and 716.2.6.1 . Penetrations of the

Section 717.5.4.1 .

Section

when

in

The elevator hoistway shall be pressurized in 
accordance with Section 909.21 .

4 
allows 
elevator 
hoistway 
pressurization to be used in lieu of enclosed 
elevator lobbies in all buildings, regardless of

-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1957 
 
interior exit stairways, which is used in lieu of 
smokeproof 
enclosures 
for 
interior 
exit 
stairways in Section 909.20.5 . The hoistway 
pressurization requirements are found in 
Section 909.21 .  
3006.4  Means of egress. Elevator lobbies shall be 
provided with at least one means of egress 
complying with Chapter 10 and other provisions in 
this code. Egress through an enclosed elevator 
lobby shall be permitted in accordance with Item 1 
of Section 1016.2 .  
❖ 
There are two requirements in this section. The 
first requires at least one means of egress from 
the elevator lobby enclosure to an exit access. 
Multiple-story 
buildings 
have 
increasing 
security concerns that often result in controlled 
access from elevator lobbies to the remainder 
of the floor. This ensures that no occupant in a 
lobby is restricted from leaving the lobby to 
access the exits on the floor. This may require 
an interior exit stairway door in the lobby if 
access is otherwise locked from the lobby. 
Elevators are not intended to be available in a 
fire situation since they are recalled during 
Phase I emergency recall, as required by this 
chapter. 
Note that 
occupant evacuation 
elevators, as addressed in Section 3008, do 
permit evacuation through the use of elevators. 
 
A common question regarding the first 
requirement is whether an interior exit stairway 
can open into an elevator lobby. Yes, an 
elevator lobby is a normally occupied space in 
the same manner that a corridor is a normally 
occupied space. As noted, if the lobby is locked 
to the rest of the floor, an interior exit stairway 
may be required in that lobby. Elevator 
hoistway doors are not permitted to open into 
an interior exit stairway. 
 
The second requirement in this section 
addresses whether occupants are permitted to 
pass through elevator lobbies in the case of a 
rated corridor. Egress through elevator lobbies 
from corridors on both sides is allowed. The 
reference to Section 1016.2 makes it clear that 
although occupants can pass through an 
elevator lobby, it is not permitted to be the only 
way to access an interior exit stairway from the 
rated corridor. This is consistent with Section 
1020.6 , which requires fire-resistance-rated 
corridors to be continuous from the point of 
entry to an exit. This wording means only one 
path needs to be continuous. This eliminates 
the allowance for a single tenant to send all 
occupants through the elevator lobby to exit the 
building.  
SECTION 3007 FIRE SERVICE ACCESS 
ELEVATOR 
3007.1  General. Where required by Section 
403.6.1 , every floor above and including the lowest 
level of fire department  vehicle access of the 
building shall be served by fire service access 
elevators complying with Sections 3007.1 through 
3007.9 . Except as modified in this section, fire 
service access elevators shall be installed in 
accordance 
with 
this 
chapter 
and 
ASME 
A17.1/CSA B44.  
Exceptions:  
1 
Elevators that only service an open or 
enclosed parking garage and the lobby of the 
building shall not be required to serve as fire 
service access elevators. 
2 
The elevator shall not be required to serve the 
top floor of a building where that floor is 
utilized only for equipment for building 
systems. 
❖ 
Section 
3007 
provides 
a 
package 
of 
requirements for what is termed a fire service 
access elevator (FSAE). A minimum of two 
such elevators is required in buildings with an 
occupied floor more than 36 m above the 
lowest level of fire department vehicle access 
in accordance with Section 403.6.1 . These 
requirements were developed based on a 
research initiative by the U.S. General Services 
Administration (GSA) and the National Institute 
of Standards and Technology (NIST), which 
focused on the development of performance 
requirements for the use of elevators for 
occupant egress and fire service access in 
buildings. Section 3007 is a byproduct of this 
research as well as preliminary information 
provided by a task group of ASME A17.1/CSA 
B44 for determining the required system 
features necessary for safe operation by 
trained fire fighters during a fire emergency.  
 
The requirements in Section 3007 are intended 
to provide a reasonable degree of safety for fire 
fighters operating the fire service access 
elevator to a location for staging fire fighters 
and equipment. In a typical situation, the 
staging area will be set up one or two floors 
below the fire. The staging location already will 
have access to an exit stairway and standpipe 
that will allow for fire-fighting operations to be 
conducted from just above the staging area.  
 
It is important to note that these requirements 
are in addition to the requirements for Phase I 
Recall and Phase II Emergency Operation. 
Therefore, these elevators would also have 
-

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1957 
 
interior exit stairways, which is used in lieu of 
smokeproof 
enclosures 
for 
interior 
exit 
stairways in Section 909.20.5 . The hoistway 
pressurization requirements are found in 
Section 909.21 .  
3006.4  Means of egress. Elevator lobbies shall be 
provided with at least one means of egress 
complying with Chapter 10 and other provisions in 
this code. Egress through an enclosed elevator 
lobby shall be permitted in accordance with Item 1 
of Section 1016.2 .

accordance 
with 
this 
chapter 
and 
ASME 
A17.1/CSA B44.

1 
Elevators that only service an open or 
enclosed parking garage and the lobby of the 
building shall not be required to serve as fire 
service access elevators. 
2 
The elevator shall not be required to serve the 
top floor of a building where that floor is 
utilized only for equipment for building 
systems.

❖ Section 
3007 
provides 
a 
package 
of 
requirements for what is termed a fire service 
access elevator (FSAE). A minimum of two 
such elevators is required in buildings with an 
occupied floor more than 36 m above the 
lowest level of fire department vehicle access 
in accordance with Section 403.6.1 . These 
requirements were developed based on a 
research initiative by the U.S. General Services 
Administration (GSA) and the National Institute 
of Standards and Technology (NIST), which 
focused on the development of performance 
requirements for the use of elevators for 
occupant egress and fire service access in 
buildings. Section 3007 is a byproduct of this 
research as well as preliminary information 
provided by a task group of ASME A17.1/CSA 
B44 for determining the required system 
features necessary for safe operation by 
trained fire fighters during a fire emergency.  
 
The requirements in Section 3007 are intended 
to provide a reasonable degree of safety for fire 
fighters operating the fire service access 
elevator to a location for staging fire fighters 
and equipment. In a typical situation, the 
staging area will be set up one or two floors 
below the fire. The staging location already will 
have access to an exit stairway and standpipe 
that will allow for fire-fighting operations to be 
conducted from just above the staging area.  
 
It is important to note that these requirements 
are in addition to the requirements for Phase I 
Recall and Phase II Emergency Operation. 
Therefore, these elevators would also have 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1958 
 
these standard features plus the additional 
package of requirements.  
 
It should also be noted that, unlike Occupant 
Evacuation Operation, ASME A17.1/CSA B44 
has no specific requirements for fire service 
access elevators beyond the Phase I/Phase II 
requirements that apply to all elevators.  
 
A fire service access elevator may be an 
elevator typically used as a service elevator, or 
a passenger elevator for general public use. 
Every floor within a building must be served by 
at least two fire service access elevators.  
 
The fire service access elevator requirements 
are intended to work in conjunction with 
requirements in other codes and standards, 
especially those that will appear in the elevator 
code, ASME A17.1/CSA B44. A key part of 
these requirements is the operation protocol for 
the fire service access elevator.  
 
It is important to stress that this elevator can be 
any elevator in the building, whether a 
passenger or service elevator. In addition, this 
elevator can also be the same elevator used for 
accessible egress in accordance with Section 
1110.4 . There are no restrictions on using the 
FSAE for this purpose. In fact, some fire 
departments may prefer this arrangement.  
 
The first exception exempts elevators that only 
serve parking garages and their associated 
lobbies from the requirement to serve as a fire 
service elevator, as FSAEs are not necessary 
in parking garages. The fire department is more 
likely to use FSAEs due to the height of the 
building. In addition, fire fighters typically are 
not willing to take an elevator past the fire floor. 
In such cases, they would generally prefer the 
use of the stairway. The second exception 
provides clarification that the fire service 
elevator is not required to serve an unoccupied 
top floor that only contains building systems 
equipment such as a mechanical room.  
3007.2  Automatic sprinkler system. The building 
shall be equipped throughout with an automatic 
sprinkler system in accordance with Section 
903.3.1.1 , except as otherwise permitted by Section 
903.3.1.1.1 and as prohibited by Section 3007.2.1 .  
❖ 
This section is intended to provide for specific 
requirements regarding automatic sprinkler 
systems associated with fire service access 
elevators.  
3007.2.1  
Prohibited locations. Automatic 
sprinklers shall not be installed in machine rooms, 
elevator machinery spaces, control rooms, control 
spaces and elevator hoistways of fire service access 
elevators.  
❖ 
This section is intended to further clarify that 
the installation of automatic sprinklers is 
prohibited in elevator machine rooms, elevator 
machine spaces, control rooms and control 
spaces associated with fire service access 
elevators. In such locations, sprinklers may be 
a detriment to the use of elevators. If sprinklers 
are located in a machine room, elevator 
machine spaces, control rooms or control 
spaces and they activate, damage to the 
elevators may occur and threaten the safety of 
fire fighters. Additionally, such spaces should 
be protected by smoke detection that would 
initiate Phase I recall prior to sprinkler 
activation (see also commentary, Section 
903.3.1.1.1, Exception 6).  
3007.2.2  
Sprinkler system monitoring. 
The sprinkler system shall have a sprinkler control 
valve supervisory switch and water-flow-initiating 
device provided for each floor that is monitored by 
the building’s fire alarm system.  
❖ 
This section allows the origin of the fire to be 
better determined and will assist in managing a 
fire emergency. In the future it is anticipated 
that such monitoring will assist in determining 
the priority for elevator locations in order to 
evacuate buildings more efficiently.  
3007.3  Water protection. Water from the 
operation of an automatic sprinkler system outside 
the enclosed lobby shall be prevented from 
infiltrating into the hoistway enclosure in 
accordance with an approved method. 
❖ 
This section provides performance-based 
language to prevent water from the discharge 
of automatic sprinklers located outside the 
elevator lobby from infiltrating the hoistway 
opening. It is important to stress that this 
requirement is only for protection from sprinkler 
system water and not water from fire fighter 
hoses. Also, the language is performance 
oriented, as there are several strategies that 
could be used to address this problem and a 
single solution may not fit in all situations. 
Solutions include concepts such as a door 
sweep, trench drains, sloped floors, curbs and 
gasketed openings, which may be used 
individually or in combination. Other methods 
may also be available to meet this intent. It 
should be stressed that the intent of this section 
is focused on the actual hoistway opening 
versus the walls surrounding the hoistway. The 
most water-sensitive aspects of the elevator 
are located at the hoistway opening. Further, 
this section does not necessarily require that 
the hoistway opening itself be protected. This 
protection could come at the entrance to the 
lobby.  
-

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1958 
 
these standard features plus the additional 
package of requirements.  
 
It should also be noted that, unlike Occupant

has no specific requirements for fire service

A fire service access elevator may be an 
elevator typically used as a service elevator, or 
a passenger elevator for general public use. 
Every floor within a building must be served by 
at least two fire service access elevators.  
 
The fire service access elevator requirements 
are intended to work in conjunction with 
requirements in other codes and standards, 
especially those that will appear in the elevator 
code, ASME A17.1/CSA B44. A key part of 
these requirements is the operation protocol for 
the fire service access elevator.  
 
It is important to stress that this elevator can be 
any elevator in the building, whether a 
passenger or service elevator. In addition, this 
elevator can also be the same elevator used for 
accessible egress in accordance with Section 
1110.4 . There are no restrictions on using the 
FSAE for this purpose. In fact, some fire 
departments may prefer this arrangement.  
 
The first exception exempts elevators that only 
serve parking garages and their associated 
lobbies from the requirement to serve as a fire 
service elevator, as FSAEs are not necessary 
in parking garages. The fire department is more 
likely to use FSAEs due to the height of the 
building. In addition, fire fighters typically are 
not willing to take an elevator past the fire floor. 
In such cases, they would generally prefer the 
use of the stairway. The second exception 
provides clarification that the fire service 
elevator is not required to serve an unoccupied 
top floor that only contains building systems 
equipment such as a mechanical room.  
3007.2  Automatic sprinkler system. The building 
shall be equipped throughout with an automatic 
sprinkler system in accordance with Section 
903.3.1.1 , except as otherwise permitted by Section 
903.3.1.1.1 and as prohibited by Section 3007.2.1 .
pages.commentary:
This section is intended to provide for specific 
requirements regarding automatic sprinkler 
systems associated with fire service access 
elevators.  
3007.2.1  
Prohibited locations. Automatic 
sprinklers shall not be installed in machine rooms, 
elevator machinery spaces, control rooms, control 
spaces and elevator hoistways of fire service access 
elevators.

❖ This section is intended to further clarify that 
the installation of automatic sprinklers is 
prohibited in elevator machine rooms, elevator 
machine spaces, control rooms and control 
spaces associated with fire service access 
elevators. In such locations, sprinklers may be 
a detriment to the use of elevators. If sprinklers 
are located in a machine room, elevator 
machine spaces, control rooms or control 
spaces and they activate, damage to the 
elevators may occur and threaten the safety of 
fire fighters. Additionally, such spaces should 
be protected by smoke detection that would 
initiate Phase I recall prior to sprinkler 
activation (see also commentary, Section 
903.3.1.1.1, Exception 6).  
3007.2.2  
Sprinkler system monitoring. 
The sprinkler system shall have a sprinkler control 
valve supervisory switch and water-flow-initiating 
device provided for each floor that is monitored by 
the building’s fire alarm system.

❖ This section allows the origin of the fire to be 
better determined and will assist in managing a 
fire emergency. In the future it is anticipated 
that such monitoring will assist in determining 
the priority for elevator locations in order to 
evacuate buildings more efficiently.  
3007.3  Water protection. Water from the 
operation of an automatic sprinkler system outside 
the enclosed lobby shall be prevented from 
infiltrating into the hoistway enclosure in 
accordance with an approved method.

❖ This section provides performance-based 
language to prevent water from the discharge 
of automatic sprinklers located outside the 
elevator lobby from infiltrating the hoistway 
opening. It is important to stress that this 
requirement is only for protection from sprinkler 
system water and not water from fire fighter 
hoses. Also, the language is performance 
oriented, as there are several strategies that 
could be used to address this problem and a 
single solution may not fit in all situations. 
Solutions include concepts such as a door 
sweep, trench drains, sloped floors, curbs and 
gasketed openings, which may be used 
individually or in combination. Other methods 
may also be available to meet this intent. It 
should be stressed that the intent of this section 
is focused on the actual hoistway opening 
versus the walls surrounding the hoistway. The 
most water-sensitive aspects of the elevator 
are located at the hoistway opening. Further, 
this section does not necessarily require that 
the hoistway opening itself be protected. This 
protection could come at the entrance to the 
lobby.  
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1959 
 
3007.4  Shunt trip. Means for elevator shutdown 
in accordance with Section 3005.5 shall not be 
installed on elevator systems used for fire service 
access elevators.  
❖ 
This section is intended to further clarify that 
the installation of a shunt trip for fire service 
access elevators is prohibited. The subject 
language is similar to the language in Section 
3008.4 for occupant evacuation elevators.  
3007.5  Hoistway enclosures. The fire service 
access elevator hoistway shall be located in a shaft 
enclosure complying with Section 713.  
❖ 
Reference is made to Section 713 of the code 
for the required fire resistance and construction 
of the hoistway enclosure. Section 713 
contains fire-resistance rating requirements, as 
well as construction requirements for shafts, 
which is typically the method used to satisfy the 
requirements for a hoistway enclosure. Section 
403.2.1.2 provides additional standards, and 
some exceptions, for hoistway construction in 
high-rise buildings, but the exceptions do not 
apply to elevator hoistway enclosures.  
3007.5.1  
Structural integrity of hoistway 
enclosures. The fire service access elevator 
hoistway enclosure shall comply with Sections 
403.2.2.1 through 403.2.2.4 .  
❖ 
This section requires additional structural 
integrity for fire service access elevator 
hoistway enclosures. See the commentaries to 
Sections 403.2.3.1 through 403.2.3.4.  
3007.5.2  
Hoistway lighting. When fire-
fighters’ emergency operation is active, the entire 
height of the hoistway shall be illuminated at not 
less than 11 lux as measured from the top of the car 
of each fire service access elevator.  
❖ 
The focus of this section is to provide 
illumination to assist fire fighters as they 
ascend in a building. The prescribed procedure 
without hoistway illumination before leaving the 
designated level (DL) is to shine a light up into 
the hoistway to try and detect smoke, flame or 
water above them. They will repeat this step 
every five floors until they safely arrive at their 
staging floor, which is two floors below the 
lowest reported floor in alarm. If fire fighters 
become trapped in a stopped elevator and 
need to self-rescue through the top of the car’s 
emergency exit, they need adequate light to 
safely escape. Hoistway lighting will make their 
life-safety maneuver much more effective. The 
illumination level is the same as that used in 
Section 1006.  
3007.6  Fire service access elevator lobby. The 
fire service access elevator shall open into an 
enclosed fire service access elevator lobby in 
accordance with 
Sections 
3007.6.1 through 
3007.6.5 . Egress is permitted through the enclosed 
elevator lobby in accordance with Item 1 of Section 
1016.2 .  
Exception: Where a fire service access elevator has 
two entrances onto a floor, the second entrance shall 
be permitted to be protected in accordance with 
Section 3006.3 .  
❖ 
All fire service access elevators must open into 
an enclosed elevator lobby meeting specific 
requirements (e.g., direct access to an exit 
enclosure and a minimum fire-resistance rating 
for lobby enclosure and door assemblies). The 
lobby must provide a reasonable degree of 
safety for fire fighters operating the fire service 
access elevator and a location for staging fire 
fighters and equipment one or two floors below 
the fire.  
 
The exception clarifies that if the fire service 
access elevator has two entrances, the second 
entrance is permitted to open into an enclosed 
or otherwise protected hoistway opening 
meeting the requirements in Section 3006.3.  
 
The code would not prohibit fire service access 
elevators and occupant evacuation elevators 
from sharing the same elevator lobby. See also 
Commentary Figure 3007.6 for a comparison 
of lobby requirements between fire service 
access elevators and elevators used for 
occupant self-evacuation.  
 
As noted in the commentary to Section 3006.1 
, this particular requirement for an elevator 
lobby is more specific and more restrictive than 
that required in Sections 3006.2 and 3006.3 . 
These 
requirements 
would 
be 
applied 
regardless of what options were chosen in 
Section 3006.3 . The same is true of the 
application of the occupant evacuation elevator 
allowance. If that allowance is chosen, the 
requirements for an enclosed elevator lobby 
would need to be in accordance with Section 
3008.6 .  
3007.6.1  
Access to interior exit stairway 
or ramp. The enclosed fire service access elevator 
lobby shall have direct access from the enclosed 
elevator lobby to an enclosure for an interior exit 
stairway or ramp.  
Exception: Access to an interior exit stairway or 
ramp shall be permitted to be through a protected 
path of travel that has a level of fire protection not 
less than the elevator lobby enclosure. The 
-

CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1959 
 
3007.4  Shunt trip. Means for elevator shutdown 
in accordance with Section 3005.5 shall not be 
installed on elevator systems used for fire service 
access elevators.

language is similar to the language in Section 
3008.4 for occupant evacuation elevators.  
3007.5  Hoistway enclosures. The fire service 
access elevator hoistway shall be located in a shaft 
enclosure complying with Section 713.

❖ Reference is made to Section 713 of the code

403.2.1.2 provides additional standards, and 
some exceptions, for hoistway construction in

3007.5.1  
Structural integrity of hoistway 
enclosures. The fire service access elevator 
hoistway enclosure shall comply with Sections 
403.2.2.1 through 403.2.2.4 .

❖ This section requires additional structural 
integrity for fire service access elevator 
hoistway enclosures. See the commentaries to 
Sections 403.2.3.1 through 403.2.3.4.  
3007.5.2  
Hoistway lighting. When fire-
fighters’ emergency operation is active, the entire 
height of the hoistway shall be illuminated at not

of each fire service access elevator.

❖ The focus of this section is to provide

safely escape. Hoistway lighting will make their

Section 1006.  
3007.6  Fire service access elevator lobby. The 
fire service access elevator shall open into an 
enclosed fire service access elevator lobby in 
accordance with 
Sections 
3007.6.1 through 
3007.6.5 . Egress is permitted through the enclosed 
elevator lobby in accordance with Item 1 of Section 
1016.2 .  
Exception: Where a fire service access elevator has 
two entrances onto a floor, the second entrance shall 
be permitted to be protected in accordance with 
Section 3006.3 .

❖ All fire service access elevators must open into 
an enclosed elevator lobby meeting specific 
requirements (e.g., direct access to an exit 
enclosure and a minimum fire-resistance rating 
for lobby enclosure and door assemblies). The 
lobby must provide a reasonable degree of 
safety for fire fighters operating the fire service 
access elevator and a location for staging fire 
fighters and equipment one or two floors below 
the fire.  
 
The exception clarifies that if the fire service 
access elevator has two entrances, the second 
entrance is permitted to open into an enclosed 
or otherwise protected hoistway opening 
meeting the requirements in Section 3006.3.  
 
The code would not prohibit fire service access 
elevators and occupant evacuation elevators 
from sharing the same elevator lobby. See also 
Commentary Figure 3007.6 for a comparison 
of lobby requirements between fire service 
access elevators and elevators used for 
occupant self-evacuation.  
 
As noted in the commentary to Section 3006.1 
, this particular requirement for an elevator 
lobby is more specific and more restrictive than 
that required in Sections 3006.2 and 3006.3 . 
These 
requirements 
would 
be 
applied 
regardless of what options were chosen in 
Section 3006.3 . The same is true of the 
application of the occupant evacuation elevator 
allowance. If that allowance is chosen, the 
requirements for an enclosed elevator lobby 
would need to be in accordance with Section 
3008.6 .  
3007.6.1  
Access to interior exit stairway 
or ramp. The enclosed fire service access elevator 
lobby shall have direct access from the enclosed 
elevator lobby to an enclosure for an interior exit 
stairway or ramp.  
Exception: Access to an interior exit stairway or 
ramp shall be permitted to be through a protected 
path of travel that has a level of fire protection not 
less than the elevator lobby enclosure. The 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1960 
 
protected path shall be separated from the enclosed 
elevator lobby through an opening protected by a 
smoke and draft control assembly in accordance 
Section 716.2.2.1 . 
❖ 
As part of the package of requirements, fire 
service access elevators need to have direct 
access to an exit enclosure. This relates to the 
fire fighters’ staging requirements during a fire. 
The exit stairways are critical to bringing water 
hoses to the fire floor. Keeping the elevators 
protected by a lobby and then locating an 
interior exit stairway that the fire fighters can 
directly access will assist in the fire-fighting 
activities. See also the commentary to Section 
3008.6.1 for a discussion regarding using the 
same interior exit stairway for both the fire 
service 
access 
elevator 
and 
occupant 
evacuation elevators.  
 
There is an exception that provides flexibility for 
direct access to an exit stairway. Essentially, it 
allows a protected path equivalent to that of the 
enclosed elevator lobby. The opening between 
the lobby and protected path still requires 
separation with a smoke and draft control door. 
This helps to keep the lobby protected from 
smoke that could infiltrate the lobby from the 
protected path. Note that there is no restriction 
on what type of doors (office, bathroom, supply 
closet, etc.) can open onto this protected path 
as long as the opening is properly protected.  
3007.6.2  
Lobby enclosure. The fire service 
access elevator lobby shall be enclosed with a 
smoke barrier having a fire-resistance rating of not 
less than 1 hour, except that lobby doorways shall 
comply with Section 3007.6.3 .  
Exception: Enclosed fire service access elevator 
lobbies are not required at the levels of exit 
discharge.  
❖ 
The fire service access elevator lobby is 
required to be enclosed by a smoke barrier 
having a minimum 1- hour fire-resistance rating 
that provides both protection from smoke and 
a level of fire resistance. This smoke barrier is 
specifically protecting the fire service access 
elevator lobby and would not be required to be 
constructed from outside wall to outside wall. 
See Section 709.4.2 , which specifically 
addresses 
smoke 
barriers 
and 
elevator 
lobbies. Note that the elevator lobby located on 
the street floor entrance to a building does not 
require an enclosed lobby. In many cases, 
such a lobby would be impractical to construct. 
In the event of a fire on the street level, the 
elevator would be directed to the alternate level 
once smoke is detected on that level.  
 
It should be additionally noted that the 
enclosure created by smoke barriers protects 
the elevator and the associated lobby from the 
building. Therefore, protection between the 
elevator lobby and the elevator is not required.  
3007.6.3  
Lobby doorways. Other than 
doors to the hoistway, elevator control room or 
elevator control space, each doorway to an enclosed 
fire service access elevator lobby shall be provided 
with a 3/4-hour fire door assembly complying with 
Section 716. The fire door assembly shall comply 
with the smoke and draft control door assembly 
requirements of Section 716.2.2.1.1 , and tested in 
accordance with UL 1784 without an artificial 
bottom seal.  
❖ 
The requirement for a 3/4-hour door is more 
restrictive than what is required for smoke 
barriers in Section 716. Section 716 and Table 
716.1(2) require a 20-minute rating for doors 
located in smoke barriers. The reference back 
to Section Section 716 is primarily related to 
how the door is to be tested and the criteria for 
acceptance. 
More 
specifically, 
Section 
716.2.1.4 requires doors used in smoke and 
draft control to be tested in accordance with UL 
1784 and installed in accordance with NFPA 
105. Additionally, a provision is included 
requiring the test be performed without an 
artificial bottom seal installed across the full 
width of the bottom of the door assembly in 
order to measure the complete leakage rate for 
the door assembly on the assumption that 
leakage could occur under the bottom of the 
door in this application. This will ensure that the 
doorways not only have an adequate fire 
protection rating for protecting the fire service 
access elevator lobbies, but also protect 
against smoke intrusion into the lobby where 
the fire service may be staging its operations 
before attacking a fire on the fire floor. This is a 
very important component of the overall 
protection package for fire service access 
elevators.  
3007.6.4  
Lobby size. Regardless of the 
number of fire service access elevators served by 
the same elevator lobby, the enclosed fire service 
access elevator lobby shall be not less than 14 m2 in 
an area with a dimension of not less than 2.4 m.  
❖ 
A minimum lobby size of 14 m2 with a minimum 
dimension of  2.4 m has been included to 
ensure that the lobby is of adequate size for 
conducting necessary fire fighter operations. 
This size was intended for one or more FSAE. 
In other words, where there are two FSAE 
required, the lobby is only required to be 14 m2 
in area. A majority of fire departments operate 
with high-rise fire attack teams composed of 
-

elevator lobby through an opening protected by a

Section 716.2.2.1 .

addresses 
smoke 
barriers 
and

Section 716. The fire door assembly shall comply 
with the smoke and draft control door assembly 
requirements of Section 716.2.2.1.1 , and tested in 
accordance with UL 1784 without an artificial 
bottom seal.

restrictive than what is required for smoke 
barriers in Section 716. Section 716 and Table 
716.1(2) require a 20-minute rating for doors

to Section Section 716 is primarily related to 
how the door is to be tested and the criteria for 
acceptance. 
More 
specifically, 
Section 
716.2.1.4 requires doors used in smoke and 
draft control to be tested in accordance with UL 
1784 and installed in accordance with NFPA 
105. Additionally, a provision is included 
requiring the test be performed without an 
artificial bottom seal installed across the full

order to measure the complete leakage rate for 
the door assembly on the assumption that

door in this application. This will ensure that the

before attacking a fire on the fire floor. This is a

protection package for fire service access 
elevators.  
3007.6.4  
Lobby size. Regardless of the 
number of fire service access elevators served by 
the same elevator lobby, the enclosed fire service 
access elevator lobby shall be not less than 14 m2 in 
an area with a dimension of not less than 2.4 m.

❖ A minimum lobby size of 14 m2 with a minimum 
dimension of  2.4 m has been included to 
ensure that the lobby is of adequate size for 
conducting necessary fire fighter operations. 
This size was intended for one or more FSAE. 
In other words, where there are two FSAE 
required, the lobby is only required to be 14 m2 
in area. A majority of fire departments operate 
with high-rise fire attack teams composed of 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1961 
 
either three or four fire fighters. The lobby 
should be large enough to accommodate one 
team preparing to enter the fire area (floor) and 
one team needing to withdraw from the fire 
area, while leaving access to the hoistway and 
exit enclosure doors clear. A minimum lobby 
size of 14 m2 is intended to meet the above 
criteria. The minimum dimension of 2.4 m is 
included to avoid a long, narrow lobby.  
3007.6.5  
Fire 
service access elevator 
symbol. A pictorial symbol of a standardized 
design designating which elevators are fire service 
access elevators shall be installed on each side of 
the hoistway door frame on the portion of the frame 
at right angles to the fire service access elevator 
lobby. The fire service access elevator symbol shall 
be designed as shown in Figure 3007.6.5 and shall 
comply with the following: 
1 
The fire service access elevator symbol shall 
be not less than 75 mm in height. 
2 
The 
helmet 
shall 
contrast 
with 
the 
background, with either a light helmet on a 
dark background or a dark helmet on a light 
background. 
3 
The vertical center line of the fire service 
access elevator symbol shall be centered on 
the hoistway door frame. Each symbol shall 
be not less than 1.9 m, and not more than 2 m 
above the finished floor at the threshold.  
❖ 
The “fire hat” symbol (see Figure 3007.6.5) is 
already used inside the elevator cab and is, 
therefore, instantly recognizable by the fire 
service. This provision is intended to aid in the 
quick identification of fire service access 
elevators and will assist the fire service in 
quickly 
identifying 
these 
elevators 
and 
beginning to use them. 
3007.7  Elevator system monitoring. The fire 
service access elevator shall be continuously 
monitored at the fire command center by a standard 
emergency service interface system meeting the 
requirements of NFPA 72.  
❖ 
As an added layer of protection, these critical 
elevators must be specifically monitored in the 
fire command center by a standard emergency 
service interface system in accordance with 
NFPA 72. More specifically, Section 7.11 of 
NFPA 72 addresses this interface. Basically, it 
requires that interface components such as 
annunciators, information display systems and 
controls, work with the needs of the fire 
department in terms of design, arrangement 
and location. The building official in the 
adopting jurisdiction must determine whether 
these fairly general requirements have been 
met. It should be noted that Annex F of NFPA 
72 contains NEMA Standards Publication SB 
30-2005, 
Fire 
Service 
Annunciator 
and 
Interface, which 
provides more 
detailed 
information about the design and layout of such 
systems. This document is written more as a 
guide and is not directly referenced in NFPA 
72.  
3007.8  Electrical power. The following features 
serving each fire service access elevator shall be 
supplied by both normal power and Type 60/Class 
2/Level 1 standby power: 
1 
Elevator equipment. 
2 
Elevator hoistway lighting. 
3 
Ventilation and cooling equipment for 
elevator machine rooms, control rooms, 
machine spaces and control spaces.  
4 
Elevator car lighting.  
❖ 
This section provides specific requirements for 
normal and standby power for fire service 
access elevators. The standby power would 
additionally include the power supply for the 
ventilation equipment for the machine room, 
machine spaces, control rooms or control 
spaces associated with the fire service access 
elevator and hoistway lighting as required in 
Section 3007.5.2 .  
3007.8.1  
Protection of wiring or cables. 
Wires or cables that are located outside of the 
elevator hoistway and machine room and that 
provide normal or standby power, control signals, 
communication with the car, lighting, heating, air 
conditioning, ventilation and fire-detecting systems 
to fire service access elevators shall be protected 
using one of the following methods: 
1 
Cables used for survivability of required 
critical circuits shall be listed in accordance 
with UL 2196 and shall have a fire-resistance 
rating of not less than 2 hours. 
2 
Electrical circuit protective systems shall 
have a fire-resistance rating of not less than 2 
hours. Electrical circuit protective systems 
shall be installed in accordance with their 
listing requirements. 
3 
Construction having a fire-resistance rating 
of not less than 2 hours. 
Exception: Wiring and cables to control signals are 
not required to be protected provided that wiring 
and cables do not serve Phase II emergency in-car 
operations.  
-

service. This provision is intended to aid in the 
quick identification of fire service access 
elevators and will assist the fire service in 
quickly 
identifying 
these 
elevators 
and 
beginning to use them. 
3007.7  Elevator system monitoring. The fire 
service access elevator shall be continuously 
monitored at the fire command center by a standard 
emergency service interface system meeting the 
requirements of NFPA 72.

❖ As an added layer of protection, these critical 
elevators must be specifically monitored in the 
fire command center by a standard emergency 
service interface system in accordance with 
NFPA 72. More specifically, Section 7.11 of 
NFPA 72 addresses this interface. Basically, it 
requires that interface components such as 
annunciators, information display systems and 
controls, work with the needs of the fire 
department in terms of design, arrangement 
and location. The building official in the 
adopting jurisdiction must determine whether 
these fairly general requirements have been 
met. It should be noted that Annex F of NFPA 
72 contains NEMA Standards Publication SB 
30-2005, 
Fire 
Service 
Annunciator 
and 
Interface, which 
provides more 
detailed 
information about the design and layout of such 
systems. This document is written more as a 
guide and is not directly referenced in NFPA 
72.  
3007.8  Electrical power. The following features 
serving each fire service access elevator shall be 
supplied by both normal power and Type 60/Class 
2/Level 1 standby power: 
1 
Elevator equipment. 
2 
Elevator hoistway lighting. 
3 
Ventilation and cooling equipment for 
elevator machine rooms, control rooms, 
machine spaces and control spaces.  
4 
Elevator car lighting.

❖ This section provides specific requirements for 
normal and standby power for fire service 
access elevators. The standby power would 
additionally include the power supply for the 
ventilation equipment for the machine room, 
machine spaces, control rooms or control 
spaces associated with the fire service access 
elevator and hoistway lighting as required in 
Section 3007.5.2 .  
3007.8.1  
Protection of wiring or cables. 
Wires or cables that are located outside of the 
elevator hoistway and machine room and that 
provide normal or standby power, control signals, 
communication with the car, lighting, heating, air 
conditioning, ventilation and fire-detecting systems 
to fire service access elevators shall be protected 
using one of the following methods: 
1 
Cables used for survivability of required 
critical circuits shall be listed in accordance 
with UL 2196 and shall have a fire-resistance 
rating of not less than 2 hours. 
2 
Electrical circuit protective systems shall 
have a fire-resistance rating of not less than 2 
hours. Electrical circuit protective systems 
shall be installed in accordance with their 
listing requirements. 
3 
Construction having a fire-resistance rating 
of not less than 2 hours. 
Exception: Wiring and cables to control signals are 
not required to be protected provided that wiring 
and cables do not serve Phase II emergency in-car 
operations.  
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1962 
 
❖ 
This section provides additional requirements 
for the protection of specific wiring and cables 
for each fire service access elevator. Typical 
building elevators do not require this level of 
protection for these wires and cables. Several 
options are provided. The first requires that 
critical circuit cables be listed in accordance 
with UL 2196 and have a fire-resistance rating 
of at least 2 hours. The second alternative is a 
listed electrical protective system with a 2-hour 
rating. The third requires that the wires and 
cables be protected by construction with at 
least a 2-hour fire-resistance rating. Note that 
protection is not required where the wiring or 
cable serves no purpose during the use of such 
elevators during Phase II operations. Phase II 
is the key fire-fighting and rescue aspect of 
elevator operation.  
3007.9  Standpipe hose connection. A Class I 
standpipe hose connection in accordance with 
Section 905 shall be provided in the interior exit 
stairway and ramp having direct access from the 
enclosed fire service access elevator lobby.  
❖ Locating the hose connection in the exit 
enclosure having direct access to the enclosed 
elevator lobby relates directly to the needs of 
fire fighters. 
 
Fire fighters typically attack a fire from the floor 
below, and this allows the shortest path for the 
fire hose to be laid. If the standpipe were 
located in the lobby itself, it would simply mean 
that the exit enclosure door would be propped 
open on the floor below the fire floor. 
 
Keeping the standpipe within the exit enclosure 
also provides a protected location for the fire 
department to gain access to the hose while 
keeping the enclosed elevator lobby, and thus 
the elevator, more protected. This particular 
issue has also been reviewed extensively by 
the ASME task group looking at the use of 
elevators 
for 
fire 
fighting, 
and 
similar 
conclusions in terms of placement within the 
exit enclosure were made.  
3007.9.1  
Access. 
The 
exit 
enclosure 
containing the standpipe shall have access to the 
floor without passing through the enclosed fire 
service access elevator lobby.  
❖ 
Access from the exit enclosure containing the 
standpipe to the floor is necessary so that the 
fire department can advance its attack hose 
onto the fire floor without opening the door 
between the enclosed elevator lobby and the 
floor, which could permit smoke contamination 
of the lobby and cause recall of the elevator(s). 
This access to the floor could be direct or 
through an access corridor or vestibule 
between the elevator lobby and the exit 
enclosure, as long as there is a smoke barrier 
enclosing the elevator lobby.  
 
An access corridor is consistent with the 
exception to Section 3007.6.1. 
SECTION 3008 OCCUPANT 
EVACUATION ELEVATORS 
3008.1  General. Elevators used for occupant self-
evacuation during fires shall comply with Sections 
3008.1 through 3008.10 .  
❖ 
This section provides options for architects to 
consider, particularly when designing tall 
buildings. More specifically, Section 403.6.2 
permits the use of all passenger elevators for 
general public use to be designated as 
occupant evacuation elevators in accordance 
with the provisions in this section. It should be 
noted that these provisions could be used in 
buildings other than high-rise buildings as long 
as all the requirements in Section 3008 are 
met. The use of these elevators is intended for 
all building occupants prior to Phase I recall 
activation of the elevators.  
 
Based on GSA/NIST research and the efforts 
of the ASME A17/CSA B44, Task Groups on 
Elevators and Fire, the use of these elevators 
by building occupants will improve overall 
building evacuation time compared to only 
utilizing 
the 
required 
exit 
stairways. 
Additionally, using elevators will also provide 
improved evacuation capability for persons 
with mobility impairments. The requirements 
provide the necessary safeguards to ensure a 
reasonable degree of safety for the use of 
elevators 
by 
occupants 
during 
building 
evacuations in normal elevator operating mode 
prior to Phase I emergency recall.  
 
The minimum lobby size is based on what is 
needed to accommodate 25 percent of the 
occupant load of the floor at 0.27 m2 per 
person, plus one wheelchair space [0.75 m by 
1.20 m)] for each 50 people (or fraction). See 
Section 3008.6.4 for more information on sizing 
lobbies.  
 
It should be noted that this section implies that 
only passenger elevators designed for general 
public use and not elevators designated as 
private elevators or freight elevators are 
required to meet all of the provisions in this 
section. However, elevators that are not for 
general public use could be used for occupant 
self-evacuation but would need to meet the 
requirements of Section 3008.  
 
There are also some instances where 
elevators were not intended to be included, 
-

SBC 201-CC-2024

elevators were not intended to be included, 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1963 
 
such as those that simply travel between the 
parking garage and the main lobby that are 
technically passenger elevators. This 
is 
addressed by Exception 2 to Section 3008.6.1 
.  
 
Although elevators are specifically being used 
for evacuation, that would not prohibit building 
occupants from taking the exit stairways during 
a fire. Occupant evacuation elevators are an 
option that will decrease the time needed for 
evacuation and reduce the likelihood of 
interfering with the fire-fighting and rescue 
operations.  
 
Note that ASME A17.1/CSA B44 has specific 
provisions related to occupant evacuation 
elevators that must also be met. These 
provisions, 
which 
are 
focused 
on 
the 
operations of the elevators, are found in 
Section 
2.27.11, 
Occupant 
Evacuation 
Operation. This includes provisions such as 
status indicators and how the elevator 
operations need to be prioritized.  
3008.1.1  
Number of occupant evacuation 
elevators. The number of elevators available for 
occupant evacuation shall be determined based on 
an egress analysis that addresses one of the 
following scenarios: 
1 
Full-building evacuation where the analysis 
demonstrates that the number of elevators 
provided for evacuation results in an 
evacuation time less than 1 hour. 
2 
Evacuation of the five consecutive floors 
with the highest cumulative occupant load 
where the analysis demonstrates that the 
number of elevators provided for evacuation 
results in an evacuation time less than 15 
minutes. 
Not less than one elevator in each bank shall be 
designated for occupant evacuation. Not less than 
two shall be provided in each occupant evacuation 
elevator lobby where more than one elevator opens 
into the lobby. Signage shall be provided to denote 
which elevators are available for occupant 
evacuation. 
❖ 
The alternative to the third stairway in Section 
403.5.2 is to use occupant evacuation 
elevators. This is a viable and more efficient 
option, but can require an excessive amount of 
standby power. In a building with many 
elevators, 
for 
example, 
standby 
power 
requirements become excessive and may be 
much more conservative than necessary 
where occupant loads are low. 
 
This 
section 
provides 
a 
reasonable 
performance-based approach while retaining 
the capacity to evacuate buildings more quickly 
than with stairs alone. Two options are provided 
to 
determine 
the 
number 
of 
occupant 
evacuation elevators necessary to meet the 
performance intent. 
 
The first option focuses on full building 
evacuation. This does not mandate full building 
evacuation but instead is a benchmark to use 
for analysis. The use of a 1-hour threshold sets 
an upper limit on evacuation time and is based 
on conclusions, reached during review of 
events such as the World Trade Center 
bombing in 1993, that buildings should not take 
longer than an hour to evacuate. The 1-hour 
criterion is consistent with the upper limit that 
the elevator industry typically uses to determine 
the use of elevators during the busier times of 
the 
day 
within 
buildings 
during 
normal 
operation. It is also consistent with the basis for 
the current code language. 
 
The second option is more closely associated 
with a more typical phased evacuation. The 15-
minute 
criterion 
is 
intended 
to remove 
occupants from the area to which the fire 
department will respond. In reviewing a number 
of resources, the time of arrival of most fire 
departments in a typical city is approximately 4 
minutes. This does not include time for set-up 
at the scene. NFPA 1710 specifically requires a 
240-second arrival time to 90 percent of the 
incidents in a jurisdiction. Again, this is only 
arrival time of the first due company. Several 
fire service officers have stated that an 
additional 10 minutes are needed to begin 
incident assessment, leading to the 15-minute 
criterion. Using the highest occupant load for 
five consecutive floors will provide a safety 
factor for the required number of occupant 
evacuation elevators. 
 
Since the initial publication of occupant 
evacuation 
elevator 
requirements, 
ASME 
A17.1 has been updated and revised to 
address occupant evacuation elevators. This 
also includes the interface with fire department 
features on elevators. Elevators can now be 
individually recalled by the fire department, thus 
leaving more elevators available for evacuation 
if necessary. ASME A17.1 also provides 
requirements for the prioritization of elevators 
during emergencies. This provides another 
underlying level of rigor to the requirements of 
this section. 
3008.1.2  
Additional exit stairway. Where 
an additional means of egress is required in 
accordance with Section 403.5.2 , an additional exit 
stairway shall not be required to be installed in 
-

Additional exit stairway. Where 
an additional means of egress is required in 
accordance with Section 403.5.2 , an additional exit 
stairway shall not be required to be installed in

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1964 
 
buildings provided with occupant evacuation 
elevators complying with Section 3008.1 .  
❖ 
This section simply explains that occupant self-
evacuation with elevators is an alternative to 
the additional exit stairway required for high-
rise buildings over 128 m in building height.  
3008.1.3  
Fire safety and evacuation plan. 
The building shall have an approved fire safety and 
evacuation plan in accordance with the applicable 
requirements of Section 404 of SBC 801. The fire 
safety and evacuation plan shall incorporate 
specific procedures for the occupants using 
evacuation elevators.  
❖ 
This section requires a fire safety and 
evacuation plan in accordance with Section 
404 of SBC 801. In high-rise buildings, 
occupant evacuation elevators are specifically 
required 
to 
be 
addressed. 
Occupant 
evacuation elevators are not necessarily 
limited 
to 
high-rise 
buildings 
and 
are 
individually addressed in Section 404. Two 
resources for discussions on high-rise building 
evacuation strategies can be found in Egress 
Design Solutions (Tubbs and Meacham 2007) 
and SFPE Engineering Guide, Fire Safety for 
Tall Buildings (SFPE/ICC 2013).  
 
SBC 201 and SBC 801 do not mandate how a 
building is to be evacuated, but instead provide 
the elements to assist in evacuation, including 
proper exit width; limitations on travel distance; 
life safety features such as sprinklers; and 
many other elements that assist occupants in 
exiting a building safely. Therefore, the way in 
which the elevators are used for evacuation 
must be tailored to the individual building and 
occupants. In most cases, when a fire occurs 
in a high-rise building, the protocol is not to 
evacuate the entire building but to phase the 
evacuation and possibly limit it only to the fire 
floor and the two floors above and below. 
Therefore, the use of elevators for evacuation 
must be coordinated with that procedure. An 
example of how such an operation would work 
is as follows: 
 
A phased evacuation of the fire zone (fire floor 
and two floors above and below the fire floor) 
is 
initiated 
with 
appropriate 
voice 
announcements 
and 
the 
elevators 
take 
occupants of these five floors to the level of exit 
discharge. While in evacuation operation, the 
elevators operate in shuttle mode between the 
five affected floors and the level of exit 
discharge, with the car buttons disabled and 
the hall call buttons arranged to tell the 
controller that there are occupants waiting on 
the other floors but not controlling the elevator 
operation. It should be noted that ASME A17.1/ 
CSA B44 has specific requirements for 
occupant 
evacuation 
elevators, 
termed 
occupant evacuation operation (OEO), that are 
consistent with the five initial floors strategy.  
 
In most cases, evacuation of the fire zone 
should be completed at about the time of fire 
department 
arrival. 
Once 
complete, 
the 
occupant evacuation elevators can be taken 
out 
of 
service 
[Fire-Fighter 
Emergency 
Operation (FEO) Phase I] until or if a decision 
for full evacuation is made by the fire 
department. Once a full evacuation decision is 
made, the occupant egress elevators are 
placed in evacuation mode [Emergency 
Evacuation 
Operation 
(EEO)] 
and 
announcements are made on the affected 
floors. The elevators then begin to move 
occupants from their floor to the level of exit 
discharge in shuttle mode, from the highest 
floors downward, so that occupants with the 
greatest distance to travel are moved first. 
Should any occupants left arrive in the lobby on 
a floor already evacuated and push the hall call 
button, a single elevator would be dispatched 
to that floor to pick them up. Alternatively, 
evacuation assistance can be provided by the 
fire service using the access elevator under 
manual (FEO Phase II) operation.  
 
It is important to note that elevators can be 
individually recalled by the fire department 
versus having to require all elevators to recall 
simultaneously. Individual switches are now 
required by ASME A17.1/CSA B44 that would 
allow egress to continue via the elevators while 
the fire department is conducting operations 
with one or two of the elevators.  
3008.1.4  
Operation. 
The 
occupant 
evacuation elevators shall be used for occupant self-
evacuation in accordance with the occupant 
evacuation operation requirements in ASME 
A17.1/ CSA B44 and the building’s fire safety and 
evacuation plan.  
❖ 
The use of an occupant self-evacuation 
elevator is only allowed until the elevator is 
recalled on Phase I. Once Phase I has been 
activated either automatically or manually, the 
continued use of the elevators for self-
evacuation would be considered unsafe and 
they would no longer be available for such use. 
After the elevator is recalled, the building 
occupants served by those elevators would 
then need to take the exit stairways (and 
persons with mobility impairments would need 
to be evacuated by fire fighters using FEO 
Phase II). In some buildings, there may be 
multiple banks of elevators. Depending on the 
fire safety and evacuation plan and the 
particular building design, occupants could 
-
pages.code_text:

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1964 
 
buildings provided with occupant evacuation 
elevators complying with Section 3008.1 .

complete, 
the 
occupant evacuation elevators can be taken 
out 
of 
service 
[Fire-Fighter 
Emergency 
Operation (FEO) Phase I] until or if a decision 
for full evacuation is made by the fire 
department. Once a full evacuation decision is 
made, the occupant egress elevators are 
placed in evacuation mode [Emergency 
Evacuation 
Operation 
(EEO)] 
and 
announcements are made on the affected 
floors. The elevators then begin to move 
occupants from their floor to the level of exit 
discharge in shuttle mode, from the highest 
floors downward, so that occupants with the 
greatest distance to travel are moved first. 
Should any occupants left arrive in the lobby on 
a floor already evacuated and push the hall call 
button, a single elevator would be dispatched 
to that floor to pick them up. Alternatively, 
evacuation assistance can be provided by the 
fire service using the access elevator under 
manual (FEO Phase II) operation.  
 
It is important to note that elevators can be 
individually recalled by the fire department 
versus having to require all elevators to recall 
simultaneously. Individual switches are now 
required by ASME A17.1/CSA B44 that would 
allow egress to continue via the elevators while 
the fire department is conducting operations 
with one or two of the elevators.  
3008.1.4  
Operation. 
The 
occupant 
evacuation elevators shall be used for occupant self-
evacuation in accordance with the occupant 
evacuation operation requirements in ASME 
A17.1/ CSA B44 and the building’s fire safety and 
evacuation plan.

❖ The use of an occupant self-evacuation 
elevator is only allowed until the elevator is 
recalled on Phase I. Once Phase I has been 
activated either automatically or manually, the 
continued use of the elevators for self-
evacuation would be considered unsafe and 
they would no longer be available for such use. 
After the elevator is recalled, the building 
occupants served by those elevators would 
then need to take the exit stairways (and 
persons with mobility impairments would need 
to be evacuated by fire fighters using FEO 
Phase II). In some buildings, there may be 
multiple banks of elevators. Depending on the 
fire safety and evacuation plan and the 
particular building design, occupants could 
-

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1965 
 
seek other elevators that have not been 
recalled. As discussed in the commentary to 
Section 3008.1.3, the use of elevators for 
evacuation in a building prior to Phase I is 
dependent on the specific building and its 
associated fire safety and evacuation plan.  
3008.2  Automatic sprinkler system. The building 
shall be equipped throughout with an approved, 
electrically supervised automatic sprinkler system 
in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 , except as 
otherwise permitted by Section 903.3.1.1.1 and as 
prohibited by Section 3008.2.1 .  
❖ 
All high-rise buildings are required to have a 
supervised sprinkler system. However, Section 
3008 can apply to buildings that may or may 
not require such systems. This section simply 
ensures that all buildings using this option 
contain supervised sprinklered systems.  
3008.2.1  
Prohibited locations. Automatic 
sprinklers shall not be installed in elevator machine 
rooms, machinery spaces, control rooms, control 
spaces and elevator hoist-ways of occupant 
evacuation elevators.  
❖ 
There are a few locations wherein sprinklers 
may be a detriment to the use of elevators for 
evacuation. If sprinklers are located in a 
machine room, machine space, control room or 
control space and they activate, damage to the 
elevators may occur and threaten the safety of 
building occupants. Additionally, such spaces 
should be protected by smoke detection, which 
would initiate Phase I recall prior to sprinkler 
activation. See also the commentary to Section 
903.3.1.1.1 , Exception 6.  
3008.2.2  
Sprinkler system monitoring. 
The automatic sprinkler system shall have a 
sprinkler control valve supervisory switch and 
water-flow-initiating device provided for each floor 
that is monitored by the building’s fire alarm 
system.  
❖ 
The monitoring required by this section allows 
the origin of the fire to be better determined and 
will assist in managing a fire emergency. Many 
of the provisions of Section 3008 are in place 
because using elevators for evacuation is a 
new concept and many safety factors were 
deemed necessary. In the future it is 
anticipated that such monitoring will assist in 
determining the priority for elevator location 
when evacuating buildings using elevators.  
3008.3  Water protection. Water from the 
operation of an automatic sprinkler system outside 
the enclosed lobby shall be prevented from 
infiltrating into the hoistway enclosure in 
accordance with an approved method..  
❖ 
This section provides performance-based 
language to prevent water from the discharge 
of automatic sprinklers located outside the 
enclosed elevator lobby from infiltrating the 
hoistway opening. It is important to stress that 
this requirement is only for protection from 
sprinkler system water and not water from fire 
fighter hoses. Also, as a single solution may not 
fit in all situations, the language is performance 
oriented, allowing for several strategies that 
could be used to address this problem. 
Solutions include concepts such as a door 
sweep, trench drains, sloped floors, curbs and 
gasketed openings, which may be used 
individually or in combination. Other methods 
may also be available to meet this intent. It 
should be stressed that this section is intended 
to apply to the actual hoistway opening versus 
the walls surrounding the hoistway. The most 
water-sensitive aspects of the elevator are 
located at the hoistway opening. Further, this 
section does not necessarily require that the 
hoistway opening itself be protected. This 
protection could come at the entrance to the 
enclosed elevator lobby.  
3008.4  Shunt trip. Means for elevator shutdown 
in accordance with Section 3005.5 shall not be 
installed on elevator systems used for occupant 
evacuation elevators.  
❖ 
A 
shunt 
trip 
has 
the 
possibility 
of 
inappropriately shutting down elevators that 
are removing people from a hazardous 
situation. Section 3008.2.1 prohibits sprinklers 
in machine rooms and machine spaces to 
prevent the shutdown of elevators due to water 
damage (see commentary, Section 3008.2.1 ).  
3008.5  Hoistway enclosure protection. Occupant 
evacuation elevator hoist-ways shall be located in 
shaft enclosures complying with Section 713.  
❖ 
Reference is made to Section 713 of the code 
for the required fire resistance and construction 
of the hoistway enclosure. Section 713 
contains fire-resistance rating requirements as 
well as construction requirements for shafts, 
which are typically the method used to satisfy 
the requirements for a hoistway enclosure. 
Section 403.2 provides additional standards, 
and 
some 
exceptions, 
for 
hoistway 
construction in high-rise buildings, but the 
exceptions do not apply to elevator hoistway 
enclosures. Also note that Section 403.2.3 
would require additional structural integrity for 
elevator hoistway enclosures in buildings more 
than 128 m in building height.  
-

contains fire-resistance rating requirements as 
well as construction requirements for shafts, 
which are typically the method used to satisfy

Section 403.2 provides additional standards, 
and 
some 
exceptions, 
for 
hoistway 
construction in high-rise buildings, but the

would require additional structural integrity for 
elevator hoistway enclosures in buildings more

pages.text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1966 
 
3008.5.1  
Structural integrity of hoistway 
enclosures. Occupant evacuation elevator hoistway 
enclosures shall comply with Sections 403.2.2.1  
through 403.2.2.4 .  
❖ 
The code requires special structural provisions 
for exit stairways and elevator shaft enclosures 
in super high-rise buildings [greater than 128 m 
in height]. It follows that the structural integrity 
requirements for super high-rise building exit 
stairway 
and 
elevator 
hoistway 
shaft 
enclosures should also apply to elevator 
hoistway 
shaft 
enclosures 
provided 
for 
occupant evacuation elevators, which are just 
as critical for life-safety protection. This new 
technology for evacuation of occupants must 
be provided with the highest level of fire 
protection that is reasonably possible in order 
to ensure that the elevators will be available 
during a fire emergency to serve their intended 
purpose of evacuating the occupants. This 
section requires that elevator hoistway shaft 
enclosures have some reasonable degree of 
physical protection to ensure that the hoistway 
shaft enclosures will remain in place during a 
fire or other emergency.  
3008.6  Occupant evacuation elevator lobby. 
Occupant evacuation elevators shall open into an 
enclosed elevator lobby in accordance with 
Sections 3008.6.1 through 3008.6.6 . Egress is 
permitted through the elevator lobby in accordance 
with Item 1 of Section 1016.2 .  
❖ 
One of the concerns when using elevators for 
evacuation is the protection of occupants while 
they wait for elevators. Therefore, an enclosed 
elevator lobby is offered to occupants. As will 
be seen, these requirements have some 
similarities to the lobbies required for fire 
service access elevators. A major difference, 
however, is that lobbies be provided for all 
passenger elevators for general public use, not 
simply for one elevator designated as a fire 
service access elevator.  
 
Since elevators used for occupant evacuation 
will be at a minimum all passenger elevators for 
general public use in buildings, it is very likely 
that one of these elevators will also be the fire 
service access elevator. Therefore, they will 
likely share the same lobby and the most 
restrictive provisions will apply. See Table 
3007.4 for a comparison of lobby requirements.  
3008.6.1  
Access to interior exit stairway 
or ramp. The occupant evacuation elevator lobby 
shall have direct access from the enclosed elevator 
lobby to an interior exit stairway or ramp.  
Exceptions:  
1 
Access to an interior exit stairway or ramp 
shall be permitted to be through a protected 
path of travel that has a level of fire 
protection not less than the elevator lobby 
enclosure. The protected path shall be 
separated from the enclosed elevator lobby 
through an opening protected by a smoke and 
draft control assembly in accordance Section 
716.2.2.1 .  
2 
Elevators that only service an open parking 
garage and the lobby of the building shall not 
be required to provide direct access. 
❖ 
As with the fire service access elevators, there 
must be direct access to an exit enclosure from 
the lobby. However, the purpose is slightly 
different. The reason is not to facilitate 
firefighting activities but instead provide an exit 
in close proximity if the elevators are no longer 
available, thus facilitating a quick evacuation. 
This also allows those who no longer want to 
wait to leave via the exit stairways without 
having to leave the protected environment 
provided by the lobby enclosure.  
 
Neither Section 3007 nor 3008 prohibits the 
interior exit stairway directly accessed by the 
elevator lobbies for occupant evacuation from 
being the same lobby that is for the fire service 
access 
elevator. 
Note 
that 
occupant 
evacuation using elevators will be much faster 
than using the exit stairways and will also 
greatly reduce the load on the exit stairways. 
This decrease in 
evacuation 
time will, 
therefore, reduce the likelihood that the 
occupants and fire fighters will interfere with 
one another. This is especially true if the 
building is only being partially evacuated.  
 
Exception 1 provides flexibility for direct access 
to an exit stairway. Essentially, it allows a 
protected path equivalent to that of the 
enclosed elevator lobby. The opening between 
the lobby and protected path does still require 
separation with a smoke and draft control door. 
This helps to keep the lobby protected from 
smoke that could infiltrate the lobby from the 
protected path. Note that there is no restriction 
on what type of doors can open onto this 
protected path as long as the opening is 
properly protected. Such doors could include 
offices, bathrooms and supply closets. 
 
Exception 2 exempts elevators that only serve 
parking garages and the lobby of the parking 
garage from the requirements of this section. 
Requiring occupant evacuation elevators to 
extend from a parking garage to the main lobby 
was not contemplated or intended to be 
addressed by the requirements for occupant 
-
pages.code_text:
CHAPTER 30—ELEVATORS AND CONVEYING SYSTEMS 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1966 
 
3008.5.1  
Structural integrity of hoistway 
enclosures. Occupant evacuation elevator hoistway 
enclosures shall comply with Sections 403.2.2.1  
through 403.2.2.4 .
pages.commentary:

in height]. It follows that the structural integrity

and 
elevator 
hoistway 
shaft 
enclosures should also apply to elevator 
hoistway 
shaft 
enclosures 
provided

draft control assembly in accordance Section

access

to an exit stairway. Essentially, it allows a

enclosed elevator lobby. The opening between

protected path. Note that there is no restriction
